metadata.seed: Yeah. So in school. I am in school for teaching, and we have to give presentations a lot, as you can imagine, on different things. And so it's something that's recurring. And what happened was I gave a really bad one one time. Yeah. I wasn't feeling very well, and I gave. I think I was just supposed tired from studying all night, and I gave a really bad presentation. And then, like, now, ever since, like, when I've tried to give subsequent presentations, I am just so nervous and I'm up there, like, sweating and, like, starting to forget what I'm gonna say. And I'm just, like, a fear of getting up there and having to get a presentation because I feel like I'm gonna bomb it.

patient.query: Yeah. So in school. I am in school for teaching, and we have to give presentations a lot, as you can imagine, on different things. And so it's something that's recurring. And what happened was I gave a really bad one one time. Yeah. I wasn't feeling very well, and I gave. I think I was just supposed tired from studying all night, and I gave a really bad presentation. And then, like, now, ever since, like, when I've tried to give subsequent presentations, I am just so nervous and I'm up there, like, sweating and, like, starting to forget what I'm gonna say. And I'm just, like, a fear of getting up there and having to get a presentation because I feel like I'm gonna bomb it.

automatic_thoughts: I gave a really bad presentation
I'm going to bomb it
I'm going to forget what I'm going to say

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like that one presentation has left a strong impression on you. What evidence do you have that you will fail again, and what evidence might suggest you could do better? Perhaps we could think about moments when you felt more confident or remembered your material.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: Yeah, that presentation... it really shook me. I just keep replaying how it went wrong in my head, and it feels like that’s all I remember. Sometimes I think I did okay in the past, like in those classes where I felt more prepared, but then I worry that maybe those were just flukes. I guess it’s hard to hold on to that confidence when I’m so focused on what could go wrong.

automatic_thoughts: replaying how it went wrong
I did okay in the past
those were just flukes
what could go wrong

llm_response.response: It sounds like the presentation left you feeling unsettled. What specific moments during the talk stand out as the most challenging? When you think about those moments, what evidence supports that they were failures, and what evidence might suggest they were just learning moments?

patient.query: Yeah, it was definitely unsettling. I just remember stumbling on my words and seeing a couple of confused faces in the audience. It felt like I was in slow motion, and I couldn't shake that feeling afterward. I keep telling myself it happens to everyone, but part of me just feels embarrassed about it. I can’t help but think that maybe they’re judging me harder than I realize, you know?

triggers: seeing confused faces in the audience
automatic_thoughts: it happens to everyone
they're judging me harder than I realize

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Disorientation as to people, time and place (finding)

llm_response.response:
It sounds like you’re noticing a lot of self‑consciousness after the presentation.  
What evidence do you have that the audience was judging you more harshly than you think?  
Are there any moments that suggest they might have been more understanding or indifferent?

patient.query: I guess... maybe I don’t have concrete evidence. It just feels like they were, you know? I mean, I just remember those few blank looks. But, yeah, there were a couple of smiles too, and I think one person even nodded along at some point. It’s just hard to hold onto that when I’m feeling so exposed. Sometimes I wish I could just ignore the negative thoughts, but they just keep creeping back in.

automatic_thoughts: I don’t have concrete evidence
it feels like they were judging me
I wish I could ignore the negative thoughts

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling exposed and unsure about what those looks meant. What evidence do you have that they were judging you, and could there be other reasons for those blank looks?

patient.query: I mean, I really don’t have solid evidence, I guess. They could have just been thinking about something else or maybe they were just processing the information. It’s just that my mind jumps to the worst-case scenarios, like they’re bored or don’t get what I’m saying. I guess I get stuck in my head and can’t see it any other way in the moment. It makes me feel really vulnerable, like I could fail any second.

automatic_thoughts: They could have just been thinking about something else
They were just processing the information
They’re bored
They don’t get what I’m saying
I get stuck in my head
I can’t see it any other way in the moment

entailment: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

It’s like my mind focuses on that one moment where I tripped over my words, and everything else fades away. I guess it just makes me feel really vulnerable, like I could just crash and burn any second.

patient.query: Yeah, I can see how it might seem like just a moment or a hiccup to someone else. Maybe to them, it was just part of the presentation and they moved on. But for me, it’s like that moment just becomes this huge deal in my head. I guess I just think that if I slip up once, it’s a sign I’m not good enough for the rest of it. I wish I could stop amplifying those little mistakes. It feels like I'm always waiting for

automatic_thoughts: it's a sign I'm not good enough
I amplify little mistakes

patient.query: I suppose I don’t really have solid evidence for that. It’s just this nagging voice in my head that keeps saying I’m not capable, you know? I think about all the times I’ve stumbled, and it just feels like it adds up. Even when people say nice things afterward, I can't fully believe them. It’s hard to shake the feeling that one mistake is a reflection of my worth, and it makes me anxious to even try again.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not capable
All the times I've stumbled add up
One mistake reflects my worth
I can't fully believe nice things people say

patient.query: Yeah, that voice can be pretty relentless. I just think about those moments when I’ve messed up and how they stand out so much more than the times I’ve done well. I mean, I can remember a few times when I got positive feedback… but it's like they get overshadowed by the mistakes. It’s frustrating because I want to focus on the good stuff, but my mind just drags me back to those failures. It feels safer to expect the worst, I think.

But when I'm up there, it's like all I can see is the mess-up, and then it spirals into this big cloud of doubt. I know they’d probably remind me of the times I did well, but it’s hard to hold onto that when I’m feeling so heavy with
